attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital for chemotherapy for invasive ductal carcinoma which has spread to her axillary lymph nodes. Two weeks ago she had an episode of cellulitis, which affected her left leg and kept her bed-bound for two days. Her medical history includes hyperlipidaemia and type 2 diabetes, which is controlled with atorvastatin and metformin. She does not drink any alcohol but has an 8 pack-year smoking history. Her current BMI is 36 kg/m².

A full blood examination has been completed prior to receiving chemotherapy.

  • Haemoglobin 115 g/L (115-165)

  • Mean corpuscular volume 79 fL (80-100)

  • White cell count 12 × 10⁹/L (4-11)

  • Platelet count 110 × 10⁹/L (150-450)

During her admission, she is considered for some form of venous thromboembolism (VTE) prophylaxis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Other than breast cancer, which of the following most affects her chance of developing a VTE?
attributes.explanation:
Patients with low or high-risk cancer, particularly those with advanced disease, are often in a pro-thrombotic state. Due to the tumour mass itself or the accompanying symptoms, these patients often exhibit abnormalities in each component of Virchow’s triad (stasis of blood flow, endothelial injury, hypercoagulability) and as a result, are at higher risk of VTE (reported odds ratio 2-9). This risk is further increased with the administration of chemotherapy.

Therapeutic guidelines recommend using the Khorana Score which is validated risk assessment tool to stratify VTE risks of patient with active cancer.

Khorana Risks for VTE in Cancer Patients:

  • Site of cancer

    • Very high risk (stomach, pancreas) +2

    • Low risk (lymphoma, gynaecological, bladder, testicular) +1

    • Other +0

  • Pre-chemotherapy platelet ≥350× 10⁹/L +1

  • Haemoglobin level <100g/L or using RBC growth factors +1

  • Pre-chemotherapy leukocyte count >11× 10⁹/L +1

  • BMI >35 kg/m² +1

Patients with a score ≥2 should be offered VTE prophylaxis with low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) or direct-acting oral anticoagulants (DOAC), if there are no contraindications.

The patient has a BMI of 36 kg/m² and raised white cell counts, and therefore meets the criteria for VTE prophylaxis. BMI ≥35 kg/m² alone has a reported odds ratio of 2.

Prolonged immobilisation (>3 days) is also associated with a higher incidence of VTE but is a weaker risk factor with a reported odds ratios of <2.

Similarly, although smoking is considered a risk factor for VTE formation, it has a low odds ratio of 1-1.4.

Pre-chemotherapy platelet are slightly below acceptable ranges and do not place the patient at greater risk of VTE formation.

Cancer patients undergoing chemotherapy over the age of 70 years old have a 2 fold increased risk of developing VTE compared to younger patients under the age of 70. Given that the patient is 65 years old in this scenario, age would be a weaker risk factor for VTE formation compared to BMI.
learningPoints: The Khorana Score is a risk assessment tool used to evaluate the risk of VTE in patients with active cancer
references: Mulder FI, Candeloro M, Kamphuisen PW, Di Nisio M, Bossuyt PM, Guman N, Smit K, Büller HR, van Es N; CAT-prediction collaborators. The Khorana score for prediction of venous thromboembolism in cancer patients: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Haematologica. 2019 Jun;104(6):1277-1287. doi: 10.3324/haematol.2018.209114. Epub 2019 Jan 3. PMID: 30606788; PMCID: PMC6545838.

Walker AJ, West J, Card TR, Crooks C, Kirwan CC, Grainge MJ. When are breast cancer patients at highest risk of venous thromboembolism? A cohort study using English health care data. Blood. 2016 Feb 18;127(7):849-57; quiz 953. doi: 10.1182/blood-2015-01-625582. Epub 2015 Nov 16. PMID: 26574606; PMCID: PMC4778298.

Qdaisat A, Wu W, Lin J, Al Soud R, Yang Z, Hu Z et al., Clinical and Cancer-Related Predictors for Venous Thromboembolism in Cancer Patients Presenting to the Emergency Department, The Journal of Emergency Medicine. 2022;58(6): 932-941. doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jemermed.2020.03.039.

Severinsen MT, Kristensen SR, Johnsen SP, Dethlefsen C, Tjønneland A, Overvad K. Smoking and venous thromboembolism: a Danish follow-up study. J Thromb Haemost. 2009 Aug;7(8):1297-303. doi: 10.1111/j.1538-7836.2009.03490.x. Epub 2009 Jun 30. PMID: 19566546.

Abdol Razak NB, Jones G, Bhandari M, Berndt MC, Metharom P. Cancer-Associated Thrombosis: An Overview of Mechanisms, Risk Factors, and Treatment. Cancers (Basel). 2018 Oct 11;10(10):380. doi: 10.3390/cancers10100380. PMID: 30314362; PMCID: PMC6209883.
